af_score_components.notes: MCP server for Jina AI platform (quality 78). Provides reader (URL to clean text), embedder (text to vectors), and reranker (relevance ranking) services. API key for production; free limited tier without auth. Simple API key. Rate limits documented by tier. Excellent for RAG pipeline agents — fetches, embeds, and ranks content in one platform. Reader handles JavaScript-rendered pages. Embeddings optimized for retrieval tasks. Good documentation and Python SDK.

security_score_components.security_notes: HTTPS enforced. API key auth (or no auth on free tier). No scope granularity. Jina AI maintained. No formal compliance certifications listed. API key via environment variable. Content fetched via Reader is processed on Jina servers — review data policy for sensitive URL fetching. Embeddings are deterministic — same input produces same vector. No auth free tier has stricter rate limits.

reliability_score_components.reliability_notes: SaaS with status page. Stable API versioning. Good migration guides. Retry guidance documented. Embedding models are versioned — model updates can change vector representations, breaking existing vector stores. Rate limits by tier — free tier very restrictive. Reader handles many page types but JavaScript-heavy SPAs may fail. Reranker improves retrieval but adds latency to RAG pipelines.

af_score_components.notes: Official Atlassian MCP server for Jira (quality 78). OAuth2 (preferred) or API key. JQL (Jira Query Language) for powerful issue filtering — must learn proprietary syntax. Issue creation, update, status transitions via workflow, comment management. Rate limits documented. Custom fields require discovery before use. Board, sprint, and backlog management. Transition IDs for status changes must be discovered per project. Excellent for project management automation agents.

security_score_components.security_notes: HTTPS enforced. OAuth2 with Jira-specific scopes or API key (Basic auth). Limited scope granularity on API keys. Atlassian maintained. SOC2/ISO27001/GDPR compliance for project management platform. API key (base64 email:token) via environment variables. OAuth preferred for granular scope control. Project data access can be restricted by Jira project permissions in addition to API-level auth.

reliability_score_components.reliability_notes: Atlassian Cloud with SLA and status page. Stable v3 API. Good migration guides (v2 to v3). Retry guidance documented. Rate limits per user per minute — batch operations require careful pacing. Issue transition IDs change per project — must discover before use. JQL complexity can cause slow queries. Custom field IDs must be discovered per project. Excellent overall reliability posture.

af_score_components.notes: No MCP server. JotForm form and survey platform. Simple API key. Form submissions, user management, form CRUD operations. Rate limits documented by plan. Form IDs required for all operations. Submission data in nested format — requires parsing. Webhooks for new submissions. HIPAA compliant forms available on Gold/Enterprise plans. Good for form automation agents — automatically processing submissions, creating forms programmatically.

security_score_components.security_notes: HTTPS enforced. Simple API key — no scope granularity. JotForm maintained. HIPAA BAA available on higher plans for healthcare use cases. API key via environment variable. Form submission data may contain sensitive PII — GDPR/HIPAA compliance required. File upload handling via form submissions must be secured. No read-only API key option.

reliability_score_components.reliability_notes: SaaS with SLA. Stable API versioning. Good migration guides. Retry guidance documented. Form submission processing not idempotent — retrying may create duplicates. Webhooks for real-time submission notifications require publicly accessible HTTPS. Rate limits by plan — free tier constrains automation. Submission data pagination required for bulk retrieval.

af_score_components.notes: MCP server for Jupyter notebook execution (quality 65). Token auth for Jupyter server. Enables LLM to create and execute notebook cells, run code, inspect outputs. Jupyter must be running with configured token. Rate limits from Jupyter server — typically no limits. Kernel execution is stateful — prior cell state affects current execution. Long-running computations block agent. Excellent for data science and scientific computing agent workflows. Error handling for kernel crashes is limited.

security_score_components.security_notes: HTTPS only if Jupyter is configured with SSL. Token auth — no fine-grained scope control. Community maintained. Jupyter can execute arbitrary Python code — significant security risk for untrusted inputs. Code execution sandbox is the kernel, not an isolated environment. Token via environment variable. JupyterHub adds user management for multi-user deployments. Never expose Jupyter to internet without authentication.

reliability_score_components.reliability_notes: Self-hosted Jupyter instance — uptime depends on local machine. Jupyter version compatibility affects MCP server behavior. No formal breaking change policy. Kernel state is cumulative — errors in prior cells affect subsequent execution. Kernel crash recovery requires restart (loses all in-memory state). Long-running computations can exceed timeout limits. Notebook output persistence requires saving notebook to disk.

af_score_components.notes: No MCP server. Apache Kafka for distributed streaming — produce/consume messages, topic management, consumer group coordination. Multiple auth methods (SASL/SCRAM, OAuth2, mTLS). Rate limits depend on broker configuration — not standardized. Consumer offsets enable at-least-once delivery with agent resumption. Topic partition management for throughput scaling. Exactly-once semantics available. Best for event-driven agent architectures. Confluent Cloud provides managed Kafka with REST API.

security_score_components.security_notes: TLS/SSL for broker connections. SASL/SCRAM-SHA-256/512, OAuth2/OIDC, mTLS options provide strong auth. ACL-based per-topic, per-consumer-group permissions. Apache community + Confluent/AWS maintained. SOC2 on managed platforms (Confluent Cloud, Amazon MSK). Credentials via environment variables. mTLS most secure for service-to-service. ACL management required for least-privilege agent access.

reliability_score_components.reliability_notes: Self-hosted or managed (Confluent Cloud, MSK). Managed platforms have SLA and status pages. Stable Kafka protocol. Good migration guides. At-least-once delivery guarantee with consumer offset management. Idempotent producers for exactly-once semantics. Consumer rebalancing can cause processing delays. Log compaction for stateful agent state storage. Excellent reliability for event-driven architectures.

af_score_components.notes: MCP integration platform (quality 62). Manages OAuth and API connections to multiple downstream services (Slack, GitHub, Google, etc.). API key or OAuth2 for Klavis itself. Reduces per-service auth setup by centralizing credential management. Rate limits from downstream services apply. Documentation is moderate. Error handling depends on downstream service. Useful for multi-service agent workflows that need unified credential management.

security_score_components.security_notes: HTTPS enforced. API key or OAuth2 for Klavis platform. Downstream service OAuth tokens managed by Klavis — trust Klavis with broad service access. Limited scope granularity. Klavis maintained. No formal compliance certifications listed. Centralizing credential management in a third-party platform creates a significant trust boundary. If Klavis is compromised, all connected service tokens are at risk.

reliability_score_components.reliability_notes: SaaS with limited public SLA information. Moderate version stability. No formal breaking change policy. Reliability depends on both Klavis platform and downstream services. OAuth token refresh for downstream services adds complexity. Klavis platform downtime blocks all downstream service access. Error propagation from downstream services through Klavis adds debugging complexity.

af_score_components.notes: No MCP server. Klaviyo email and SMS marketing platform. Simple API key (private key for server-side). Rate limits documented per endpoint. Profiles, events, lists, campaigns, flows via API. Event tracking for ecommerce automation. Batch profile updates available for efficiency. Klaviyo-specific metric/event naming. Cursor-based pagination. Webhook support for campaign and flow events. Good for ecommerce marketing automation agents.

security_score_components.security_notes: HTTPS enforced. Simple private API key — no scope granularity. Klaviyo maintained. SOC2/GDPR/CAN-SPAM compliance for marketing platform. API key via environment variable. Customer marketing data contains PII — GDPR compliance required for EU contacts. Email/SMS sending via API can generate large volumes — monitor for abuse. No read-only API key option.

reliability_score_components.reliability_notes: SaaS with SLA and status page. Stable v3 API. Good migration guides (v1/v2 to v3). Retry guidance documented. Rate limits per endpoint — varies by resource type. Batch profile upserts reduce rate limit pressure. Event tracking idempotent with unique_id. Email sends not idempotent — use campaign ID for deduplication. Cursor pagination for large list management.

af_score_components.notes: MCP server for Kong API Gateway management (quality 72). API key + RBAC for admin operations. Manage routes, services, plugins, consumers. Rate limits configured per service in Kong. RBAC (Kong Enterprise) required for fine-grained admin access. Plugin management enables adding auth, rate limiting, caching to upstream APIs. Declarative configuration (deck) is preferred over API for production. Kong is complex to configure correctly — understand proxy vs admin API distinction.

security_score_components.security_notes: HTTPS for admin API (Kong Enterprise) — community edition may use HTTP. API key + RBAC for admin operations. Kong Enterprise provides RBAC; Community has limited access control. Kong maintained. No formal compliance certifications listed (Enterprise may have). API key via environment variable. Admin API exposure is high-risk — must be network-isolated. RBAC roles limit blast radius for agent operations.

reliability_score_components.reliability_notes: Self-hosted or Kong Cloud. Kong Cloud has SLA; self-hosted operator-managed. Stable versioned Admin API. Good migration guides. Retry guidance documented. Route and service operations are idempotent with declarative config. Plugin ordering matters — incorrect ordering can break traffic flow. DB-less mode (declarative config) is more reliable than DB-backed mode for agents.

af_score_components.notes: MCP server wrapping kubectl for Kubernetes management (quality 72). Kubeconfig for auth — agents need valid kubeconfig with appropriate RBAC permissions. Rate limits from Kubernetes API server. Resource management, deployment scaling, pod status, logs. kubectl commands have high blast radius — can delete resources, scale to zero. Namespace-scoped operations require explicit namespace specification. ReadOnly vs ReadWrite kubeconfig contexts for safety. Good for Kubernetes automation agents with proper RBAC.

security_score_components.security_notes: TLS via Kubernetes cluster. Kubeconfig with RBAC for fine-grained resource permissions. Kubernetes RBAC supports per-resource, per-verb, per-namespace controls — excellent least-privilege design. Community maintained. Kubeconfig stored in ~/.kube/config or env var — must protect. ReadOnly service account recommended for monitoring agents. Never use cluster-admin for agents. RBAC design is critical for safe Kubernetes automation.

reliability_score_components.reliability_notes: Self-hosted Kubernetes — uptime depends on cluster health. Kubernetes API stable across minor versions. Breaking changes managed across major versions (e.g., API deprecation). kubectl retry mechanisms via kube-apiserver rate limiting. Kubernetes operations are generally idempotent (apply is declarative). API server rate limiting requires backoff. Pod restart policies provide implicit error recovery. Complex operations (rolling deployments) require state tracking.

af_score_components.notes: MCP server for kubefwd (Kubernetes port forwarding utility) (quality 62). Kubeconfig auth. Enables agents to forward Kubernetes service ports for local access. Useful for development workflows — allows agents to reach cluster services without external exposure. Requires active cluster connection. Documentation limited. Error handling for connection drops is basic. Low security score (40) reflects open port forwarding risk. Not suitable for production automation — development tooling only.

security_score_components.security_notes: TLS via Kubernetes for cluster auth. Port forwarding opens local ports — any local process can connect to forwarded services. Kubeconfig auth for cluster access. Community maintained with limited security review. Kubeconfig in environment variable or default location. Port forwarding bypasses cluster network policies for external access — significant security consideration. Development tool only — never use in production agent workflows.

reliability_score_components.reliability_notes: Self-hosted development tool with no SLA. Unknown version — actively maintained but may have breaking changes. No formal change policy. Port forward connections drop on network interruption — must reconnect. Kubernetes service availability affects port forward stability. Not suitable for long-running production agent workflows. Good for development sessions where manual reconnection is acceptable.
